doc.title: Diluted EPS — options (treasury-stock method)
doc.event: Ives Corp.: income available to common $490,000, WASO 200,000 (basic EPS $2.45). Outstanding: 10,000 stock options with a $20 exercise price; the stock's average market price was $25. Compute diluted EPS.

computationPaths.narration: Treasury-stock method: pretend all 10,000 options exercise → proceeds 10,000 × $20 = 200,000. Pretend the company uses that cash to buy back shares at the $25 AVERAGE market → 8,000 reacquired. Net new shares = 10,000 − 8,000 = 2,000. No numerator adjustment — options avoid no interest.

variants.label: Out of the money — antidilutive, EXCLUDE

computationPaths.narration: Strike $30 against a $25 average: the pretend proceeds (300,000) would buy back 12,000 shares — MORE than the 10,000 issued. Negative incremental shares would RAISE EPS, which diluted EPS never allows. The options are antidilutive: excluded entirely; diluted = basic = $2.45.

memorize.body: Treasury-stock method: incremental shares = options − (options × strike ÷ AVERAGE market price). Numerator untouched. In the money (strike < avg market) → dilutes; otherwise exclude.
Shortcut: incremental shares = options × (avg market − strike) ÷ avg market. Here: 10,000 × 5/25 = 2,000. Same answer, one line.
AVERAGE market price for the period — not year-end price. And options join denominators only; convertibles are the ones that touch the numerator.

questions.prompt: 10,000 options, $20 strike, $25 average market. Income to common 490,000; WASO 200,000. Diluted EPS =

distractors.feedback: 2.33 adds all 10,000 option shares (490,000 ÷ 210,000) and ignores the pretend buyback. The 200,000 of exercise proceeds repurchases 8,000 shares at the $25 average — only the net 2,000 dilute: 490,000 ÷ 202,000 = 2.43.
2.45 (= basic) is right ONLY when the options are out of the money. At a $20 strike vs $25 average they're in the money and must dilute — 2,000 incremental shares → 2.43.
